title: The Most Interesting Displays In The World!

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So MacBooks don't have touchscreens. But what if they did?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This thing here is called the Magic Screen by Intricate. It's a touch sensitive flexible glass that snaps onto a MacBook with magnets, plugs in via USB type C, and immediately gives that Mac a full corner-to-corner touchscreen.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And it works exactly how you'd expect. You can scroll up and down websites and in apps and poke around, do a little bit of photo editing. Of course all the iPhone mirroring in macOS Tahoe works even better since now you're controlling the app as it was meant to be controlled with your fingertip.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So this is obviously a bit of a hack to make this possible. It's actually literally pretending to be a trackpad to the laptop. But as far as hacks go, this is actually it's pretty well thought out.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: They've thought about the wobble that comes with touching the screen and made this accessory folio case for it that holds the display up nicely. They've thought about travel with it, so it tucks into here really cleverly to make it go with you everywhere the MacBook goes.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You also obviously don't want to accidentally close the glass into the laptop and maybe break it, so they designed this corner USB type C port to also be a stopper so it literally doesn't let you make that mistake.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: They even made this stylus accessory as well, so you can use this off of the laptop separately like a transparent Wacom tablet, and that gives it hovering and pressure sensitivity, and it has a 100-hour battery life.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the elephant in the room with all of this is the MacBook Pro update we're expecting later this year. If you haven't already heard, it's we're expecting a redesign with an OLED and a touchscreen.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so knowing that, that kind of makes this product feel like it has a ticking expiration date. But I do think it's worth considering not everybody who kind of wants a touchscreen is going to just have to go out and upgrade and buy a new $5,000 laptop to get it, especially when their M2 or M3 is still working perfectly well and probably still will for a couple more years.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And the thing is, I think when that MacBook Pro comes out, it's actually going to make this product better too. Because as of right now, macOS is simply not built for touchscreens. Like they've never had touchscreens, the whole thing is designed for mouse and keyboard input.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so yeah, some parts kind of look like they're starting to lean more towards touch controls like control center and some of these insane controls, but that's a rant for another day.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the point is, when they do come out with that touchscreen laptop, they will also have to update macOS to be more touch friendly in some ways, like some larger touch targets and some more adaptive UI. And so those things will make it to all of these, and that will make this easier to use.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So things like the stoplight window controls, which are definitely too small to consistently press on the first try, that could get way easier. Grabbing the top of a window to move it around, things like that won't be such impossibly small touch targets anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So that's the bright side. They say they're planning on shipping a $150-ish version after a Kickstarter later this year, which definitely makes me nervous, but I'm wishing them luck. I'm still not the biggest touchscreen laptop person myself, but I think there's a...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: some people out there who will love this. I'm going to link it below. It's definitely dope tech.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now, this video is all about dope tech that is displays. And so this one by definition maybe pushing that limit just a little bit because it's a volumetric display, aka a hologram.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now, we've already done an entire Studio channel video all about this and how it works and why it technically isn't actually a hologram. But you know what? The technicality doesn't matter as much. The idea is that it's creating the effect of a hologram, which is this cool object displayed in 3D space that people can gather around and view it almost at 360 degrees. And the way that it's creating this effect is actually kind of insane.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, this is what it looks like when it's off, actually. Right? So you can see here, it's actually two displays back to back and rotating around this center axis here. And you can actually see individual pixels because it's not that super high resolution of a screen. But that's going to make a lot more sense in a second.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So like you've probably seen one of those spinning fan displays before. If you spin that one line of pixels around really, really fast, you can make a circle. And so if you pulse those LEDs so that they're in the exact same position each time they light up, you get a floating dot in space, like a pixel. So when you stack a bunch of these lines, that's when you start to add this Z-axis of depth. But that's also when it gets incredibly complicated.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, now it's back on again. This thing spins at 900 RPM, which is about 15 times every second. And as it spins that fast, you can see when it lights these things up, instead of just one dot or one line at a time, which would look like a floating slice, now it has to take 3D objects and display all of the slices next to each other with perfect timing so that as the panels spin around, it creates the illusion of 3D objects with actual volume, sort of hovering still in space.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And this is an incredibly complex feat of a ton of processing because since a full rotation is divided into 480 unique slices, that means just to do 30 FPS video, you have to update these displays 7,200 times per second. 7,200 Hertz refresh rate on these displays just to show this object at 30 frames per second. That is insane. And that's also why it's not the highest resolution display in the world, because there's already a ton of compute going on just to make this possible.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also fun fact, there's even a little bit more processing headroom necessary for error correction because these motors don't necessarily spin at exactly 900 RPM and so it has to make sure these things all align up perfectly. But when they do, well, you're going to have to take my word for it because it's incredibly hard to capture, but it it does look great. It looks so cool in person. Obviously, the display is partially covered up here by a glass dome, partially for safety.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also partially to keep the display spinning with less air resistance inside. So there's a lot going on here. But yeah, there's specialized software to show these images and 3D objects made specifically to be shown on volumetric displays. And I just haven't seen anything else that looks quite like it. If you're interested in more on recording holograms or why someone might want to beat me up for even calling this a hologram in the first place, definitely watch the studio video linked below. It is one of the most interesting pieces of tech that's ever come through the studio. Definitely dope tech.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Alright, so I know I just got rated an S-tier chef by Nick DiGiovanni and that's cool and everything, small flex, but I also appreciate when cooking is extremely easy. And that's where our video sponsor, Chef iQ, comes in.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: See this piece here is the tech we're all familiar with, but it's connected to this mini oven and it turns it into a fully connected cooking setup. Just the oven by itself is already sick. You can just pop onto the screen whatever you want to cook, fish, chicken, steak, or even eggs, and it knows how to cook it perfectly.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It can air fry, it can bake, broil, dehydrate, whatever. There's literally 10 plus cooking modes built in and it does all of them well so you can replace multiple things on your countertops. And if we go ahead and check on these, perfectly toasted. Something else this is really good at by the way is eggs. Might not be expected, but it actually comes with this egg tray. You can crack an egg in six of these and make fried eggs basically perfectly every single time however you want.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Something else to note people don't really think about is this thing still gets up to 500 degrees, but it is way smaller than a traditional oven. And so it preheats way faster, so I've been using my traditional oven less. But even better, pair it with the IQ sense thermometer and now it knows when the food is done. So like if it's chicken, it knows what the internal temps should be and it nails that every single time.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And of course there's an app too. So now if I have something going for much longer, I totally leave the room and then I can check on progress at any time and of course get a notification when it's actually done. So if you too want to be an S-tier chef, but way easier, you can save 40% with code MKBHD at the link in the description.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And yes, I did say 40%. You're welcome. Alright, on to the next item.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Okay, so this monitor. It almost looks reasonable because of how far away from it I'm sitting right now, but it's not. This is a 52 inch ultra-wide 6K slightly curved 120 hertz Thunderbolt monitor from Dell.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And Dell actually came out with a bunch of interesting displays this year, but this, in my opinion, is the most interesting one. And the reason it's interesting to me is because I've been a dual monitor person for a long time. You've seen my desk setups. This is the first single monitor that I actually feel like I could use, and I've tried a lot of ultra-wides, but the first single monitor I think I could actually use to replace dual monitors and like go all the way and not lose a beat.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So first of all, 6K resolution, of course. That's 6144 by 2560. So it's not 8K or anything, but it's still enough pixels that it works out to...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: be basically the same pixel density as a 27 inch 4k monitor so 129 pixels per inch.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Text is still super sharp, corners are crisp,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: so you could sit much closer to it without it breaking down at all.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: it just looks really good, especially for not being an OLED.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It has these thin bezels all the way around, it's a slightly anti-glare finish,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and it's an IPS panel, 100% sRGB and 99% of DCI-P3 color.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's not like the absolute highest end color grading type of panel,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: but it is $3,000 and for all that money you do get a very physically impressive and imposing display.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the feature that makes this really interesting, especially for someone who uses multiple machines, is this has a built-in KVM switch for up to four computers at once,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and then a bunch of really nice software surrounding it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So you've got Thunderbolt 4, there's two HDMI 2.1 ports, two DisplayPort 1.4 ports,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: you can have multiple machines plugged in and then switch between them with one keyboard and mouse.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that's basically what it's designed around. So instead of, you know, picture in picture, they have these picture-by-picture modes

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: with screen partitioning into a bunch of different arrangements. This is what makes it a viable replacement for dual displays.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You can even put everything on a single display even from multiple machines.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's a multi-monitor setup on one monitor all at once.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And the slight curve is also really nice. You can kind of see here at the edges,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: there are some much more dramatically curved ultrawide monitors and that's... it looks cool and everything,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: but this is a little closer to mimicking the actual angle that I have my dual displays in front of me at my desk.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Because when you have two big displays next to each other, having them flat just ends up making the corners really far away from you when you're sitting in the middle.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the slight curve just helps just... it's a subtle thing, but it helps keep things a little bit more directly in front of you so there's less head turning.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that Thunderbolt 4 connection, that'll do the 140 watts of power, so you plug it into your laptop through this single cable,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: you're charging, you're displaying, you can get two and a half gig Ethernet through there all at once, it's great.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So for the person who wants one display to rule them all for their entire desk setup,

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: this is probably the one right now. It might cost two displays, but it kind of is two displays.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So I like it. It's Dope Tech.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Okay, let me ask you a question: smart glasses or VR headset?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Which one are you more into? Because a smaller wearable pair of glasses you can wear all day

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and then it gives you convenient stuff like a heads-up display with notifications and directions and translations

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and it looks inconspicuous.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Or there's the full-on headset, which has the nicest displays but it completely takes over your vision

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: for the most immersive detailed high-resolution content experience.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: If you answered

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: neither, but you still kind of like some parts of both?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Well perfect, then meet Project Aura. And this is an exclusive brand-new first look here

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and actually I can't think of a better way to explain it than just basically being right in between.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: They're a collab between Xreal, Google, and Qualcomm to bring this to life

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and I don't know if you've seen some of the other Xreal glasses but they're actually very simple.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The idea is you essentially have a stationary display that you put in front of your face

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and now you have like a hundred inch display effectively wherever you go.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: anywhere you want to go. It's clever, but that was very much plug and play with other devices. This new Project Aura runs Android XR, which means a couple things.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: First of all, it's its own computer, it's wired to this smartphone-sized puck here by USB-C.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then it has its own sensors around the frame so that it can bring in your environment and anchor the graphics in physical space.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So those two cameras on the corners on the outside are just for tracking and then there's a single RGB camera in the middle.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So when you fire these up, you look like this, but you see this.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Android XR locked hovering in space in front of you. And then you can do everything here that you could do in the VR headset.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You can open any of those Android apps, watch videos, move around virtual environments.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And they're pretty good-looking displays too, to my eye, 1920 by 1200 per eye. They're Micro-OLED at 72 FPS.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But obviously the 70-degree field of view is nice for glasses, but that's going to be less than a full-on VR headset.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The key though is it's going to be way cheaper and lighter than a full-on VR headset, so 91 grams to be exact.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so you're sort of you're trading off the immersiveness of a full-on headset for the ability to see a bit more around you, looking through real glass to see the actual environment behind the UI.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And so since they're lighter, they end up a bit more comfortable and you might even be tempted to get up and walk around in them or just wear them for a longer period of time than you would have worn a headset.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's this feature that lets you while you're watching a video pin it to the corner of your vision so you can walk around with it like a little mini video player in a little HUD in the corner of your vision.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's kind of perfect for doing the chores and walking around or doing the dishes while listening to the Waveform podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like before to take a video with you in a VR headset you'd like have to pinch it and hold it and then hold it while you're walking around literally taking it with you.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So they've clearly thought about how much more likely you are to do that with these glasses than a regular headset.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then if you ever just want to watch a video or movie with just a black background instead of the environment around you, the glass has an electrochromic tint built in so you can black them out with the click of a button, which is pretty fire.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The glass all the way clear is like the equivalent of a light pair of sunglasses. It's not totally clear, but you can still definitely see the real world with the AR elements locked in.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then all the way tinted is basically pitch black.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So when this actually goes on sale, the pitch is actually going to be split computing, which is basically just the idea that you have the compute in the glasses and the compute in the puck as separate and that comes with some unique advantages.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like the puck is running the new Snapdragon AR2 Gen 1 chip inside, so it's high end now and has a power button that doubles as a fingerprint reader and the whole front of it is a touchpad.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: That's doing most of the compute, it runs the OS, it runs all the AI stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But when someday maybe that gets outdated, you can upgrade just the puck and then plug it in by USB type-C to the frames.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Or maybe someday in the future you decide, yeah, I kind of want better frames. I want a wider field of view...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: ...two or more resolution or whatever it is, some new frames come out. Okay, then you can upgrade just the frames and keep the same computer.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I was already super impressed just with the tracking from such few sensors and cameras and the super low latency with this setup as it is.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Literally three milliseconds on this setup versus around 12 milliseconds on the Vision Pro. But yeah, the idea is this is somewhat modular, so you can update it one piece at a time in the future, and that alone is very clever.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Literally less than a year ago was my video titled, "Wait, Smart Glasses are Actually Good Now?" with the latest AR glasses from Meta, but like eight months later we already have all this choice, all these different ideas of how these companies want to execute on face computer stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that's fascinating, and it is dope tech. Thanks for watching. Catch you guys in the next one. Peace.

highlights: The Magic Screen by Intricate offers a "hack" for MacBook users by using magnets and a USB-C connection to add full touchscreen functionality and stylus support to existing laptops.
A specialized volumetric display creates the illusion of a 3D hologram by spinning panels at 900 RPM, requiring a massive 7,200 Hz refresh rate to produce a stable 30 FPS image.

Dell’s 52-inch 6K ultra-wide monitor is designed as a complete dual-monitor replacement, featuring a built-in KVM switch and software that can partition a single screen for up to four different computers.
Project Aura introduces a "split computing" model for AR glasses, separating the processing hardware into a pocket-sized puck to allow for a lightweight 91-gram headset and modular future upgrades.

Upcoming AR technology like Android XR features electrochromic glass, which can transition from transparent to pitch black to toggle between augmented reality and a fully immersive viewing experience.
summary:
The transcript highlights several innovative display technologies, ranging from practical productivity tools to experimental hardware. Marques Brownlee first showcases the "Magic Screen," a magnetic, flexible glass overlay that adds touchscreen functionality to existing MacBooks. While Apple is rumored to be developing an official touchscreen Mac, this accessory serves as a functional "hack" that bridges the gap, especially as macOS begins to incorporate more touch-friendly UI elements. For professional setups, Brownlee reviews Dell’s 52-inch ultra-wide 6K monitor, which is designed to replace dual-monitor configurations. It features high pixel density, a built-in KVM switch for controlling up to four computers, and a subtle curve to improve ergonomics.

The video also explores the future of immersive and specialized displays, starting with a volumetric "holographic" display. This device uses rotating LED panels spinning at 900 RPM to create the illusion of 3D objects in physical space, requiring immense processing power and a 7,200Hz refresh rate to function. Finally, Brownlee introduces "Project Aura," a collaboration between Xreal, Google, and Qualcomm. These AR glasses run Android XR and utilize a "split computing" design, where the processing is housed in a separate, smartphone-sized puck. This modular approach allows for a lighter, more comfortable headset that features electrochromic tinting and spatial anchoring, representing a middle ground between simple smart glasses and bulky VR headsets.
